Interpolate bivariate data on grids or point locations

Source: R/interpolate.R
interpolate.Rd

`interpolate()` provides a unified interface for interpolating two-dimensional data from regular grids, irregular grids, or point locations to new spatial locations. The default method is `"bilinear"`.

Usage

interpolate(x, y, z, ...)

# Default S3 method
interpolate(
  x,
  y,
  z,
  xout,
  yout,
  method = "bilinear",
  extrap = FALSE,
  control = list(),
  ...
)

Arguments

x, y

Coordinates describing the input data. Supported input formats are:

  • a regular grid, given as increasing numeric vectors, with `dim(z)[1:2] == c(length(x), length(y))`;

  • an irregular grid, given as numeric matrices with the same dimensions as `z`;

  • point data, given as numeric vectors of the same length as `z`.

z

Numeric values to interpolate. `z` must be a matrix for a single spatial field, or an array whose first two dimensions match the spatial dimensions implied by `x` and `y`.

...

Additional arguments passed to the underlying interpolation helper.

xout, yout

Coordinates of the target locations. Supported output formats are:

  • a regular grid, given as increasing numeric vectors;

  • an irregular grid, given as numeric matrices of identical dimensions;

  • paired point locations, given as numeric vectors of equal length.

method

Interpolation method. Currently supported methods in the shared implementation are `"bilinear"`, `"nearest"`, and `"akima"`. Not all methods support all combinations of input and output geometry; see Details.

extrap

Logical; if `TRUE`, allow extrapolation outside the input domain when supported by the selected method. If `FALSE`, unsupported target locations are returned as `NA`.

control

A named list of method-specific control options. Common entries include:

`mask`

Optional mask used by bilinear interpolation. Values equal to `0` are treated as missing.

`link`

Optional link function name used to transform `z` before interpolation and back-transform it afterwards via [stats::gaussian()].

`linear`

Logical; for `"akima"` interpolation, request linear rather than Akima interpolation.

Additional named entries are passed to the underlying Akima routines; see Details.

Value

A list with components:

`x`, `y`

The requested output coordinates, returned in the same form as supplied by `xout` and `yout`.

`z`

Interpolated values. For regular-grid output this is typically a matrix of dimension `length(xout) x length(yout)`. For irregular-grid output, it has `dim(xout)`. If the input `z` has additional trailing dimensions, they are preserved after the two spatial dimensions.

Details

The function accepts a spatial field as a matrix, or a higher-dimensional array whose first two dimensions represent space. When `z` has additional trailing dimensions, interpolation is applied independently to each layer and the extra dimensions are preserved in the output.

Inputs are classified internally as one of three spatial data layouts: regular grids, irregular grids, or point data.

Missing values in gridded inputs are handled differently depending on the interpolation method. For Akima-based interpolation, complete cases are extracted and treated as point data. For bilinear and nearest-neighbour interpolation, support is currently limited to regular-grid origins.

In the current implementation:

  • `"bilinear"` is implemented for regular-grid origins and regular-grid or irregular-grid targets;

  • `"nearest"` is implemented for regular-grid origins and regular-grid or irregular-grid targets;

  • `"akima"` supports regular grids, irregular grids, and point data, and is the method that most clearly supports paired point output;

  • bilinear and nearest-neighbour interpolation are not implemented for irregular-grid or point-data origins.

For Akima-based interpolation, `control` may include arguments used by the underlying Akima routines, such as `duplicate`, `dupfun`, `nx`, `ny`, `jitter`, `jitter.iter`, `jitter.random`, and `remove`.

See also

[akima::interp()], [akima::interpp()]

Examples

if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{
data(volcano)
x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = nrow(volcano))
y <- seq(0, 1, length.out = ncol(volcano))
xout <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 3 * nrow(volcano))
yout <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 3 * ncol(volcano))

out <- interpolate(x, y, volcano, xout, yout, method = "bilinear")
str(out)
} # }
